narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.9% chance that you would not pass. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger projected outcomes. The model's decision for you, as a Black male applicant, hinged most on your academic performance in school, particularly your Year 3 grades placing you in the second decile—well below the typical fifth or sixth decile for most students—which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling potential challenges in handling rigorous legal coursework. Similarly, your Year 1 grades also in the second decile, again lower than average, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor driving the risk upward. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction somewhat toward success compared to students with weaker GPAs. Other aspects like your middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 32 (below the usual 36.7 average), and full-time student status played lesser roles but didn't offset the primary academic red flags. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often rank in higher deciles early on and show steadier academic climbs, your profile stood out for its lower starting point, which the model sees as a key hurdle for bar exam success. We understand this feedback might feel tough, especially given the barriers many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic environments. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your grades to climb those school deciles—perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework—and retake the LSAT to aim closer to or above the average score. Building a stronger undergraduate record retrospectively isn't possible, but highlighting any upward trends or extenuating circumstances in a new application could help. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, to demonstrate resilience. With deliberate steps, you can reshape your profile for better odds next time.
